title: 1.0 TC4x - CPU - Use Case 1 -Dedicated memory using system address map

content.text: This page is to analyze the SMs needed for Dedicated memory using system address map
This use case considering the scenario where a TriCore CPU with Lockstep (LS) monitoring enabled is accessing any of its dedicated memory resources using the System Address Map in linear mode

The CPU can access its dedicated memory resources to execute code, to load or store variables and to load constants. In some cases, an instruction fetch request or data load or store request will be routed outside the CPU through the SRI bus and back into the CPU. A load request for a variable stored in PSPR would be an example for this.

Code can be fetched from the non-volatile memory (PFLASH or PRRAM) through the dedicated Core Flash Interface (CFI) or from the local RAM. The CPU can execute the code by following the program flow as stored in the memory but also as a response to an NMI coming from the SCU or to an interrupt service request arriving from the interrupt router. Additionally, the CPU can send interrupt requests to the IR associated with events related to the system timer (STM) inside of it

The selection of the address map mode is controlled by the SCU through the “SOTA control” interface. An error in SCU SOTA control logic can lead to wrong software execution. This applies even for devices that do not have SOTA support as the interface to SCU is implemented anyways

content.local_path: _images/1.0 TC4x - CPU - Use Case 1 -Dedicated memory using system address map/Dedicated memory using system address map.jpg

rows: SM[HW]:CPU:CFI_LOCKSTEP

The lockstep detects permanent and transient faults in the operation of a hardware element. Two symmetrical hardware modules perform duplicate operations in lockstep (or delayed by a fixed period) and the results are compared. Any mismatch results in an error condition signaled by an alarm .
This safety mechanism is enabled after application reset
NA
If this safety mechanism detects a fault, the CFI generates a CFI lockstep mismatch alarm
SM[HW]:CPU:CFI_LOCKSTEP_SCC

The lockstep incorporates a hardware-based self-test. The self-test function sequentially injects faults into each comparator node. The lockstep detects that the fault was routed to the correct comparator node. If the fault was not routed to the correct comparator node or a real fault was detected, the lockstep generates an alarm
This safety mechanism is enabled after application reset
NA
If this safety mechanism detects a fault, the CFI generates a CFI lockstep mismatch alarm
SM[HW]:CPU:CFI_PFRWB_ADDRESS_MONITOR

The address logic used in the interface between the CPU and the NVM is protected by a safety mechanism that checks that the returned data corresponds to the requested address. With each returned data, the PFRWB returns a copy of the requested address named safe_address. The CPU CFI HW uses this safe address to compute a data phase ECC signature. If the CPU uses this data packet, another signature based on the requested address is computed by the CPU HW (code fetch interface or data read interface) and then both signatures are compared
This safety mechanism is always enabled.
NA

If the CPU uses this data packet and detects a mismatch in signatures, the CPU generates an alarm and an exception: For code fetch related packets, the CPU generates a PIE trap For data read related packets the CPU generates a DIE trap
SM[HW]:CPU:CFI_WAIT_STATE_PROTECTION

The CPU uses an error correction code to detect a fault in the wait state configuration of the NVM to protect the NVM read timings. The wait state configuration is protected using an Error Detection Code (EDC) mechanism with single bit error and double bit error detection capabilities
This safety mechanism is always enabled.
NA
If the CPU detects a single or double bit error in the configuration of the program flash wait state, the CPU generates a CPU CFI wait state alarm
SM[HW]:CPU:SWAP_MAP_SELECTION
The system address map may be configured for linear address map, SOTA address map A or SOTA address map B. The CPU monitors the swap map selection
This safety mechanism is always enabled.
NA
If the CPU detects an illegal value in the swap map selection, the CPU generates a CPU swap map selection alarm
SM[HW]:CPU:TRICORE_LOCKSTEP

The lockstep detects permanent and transient faults in the operation of a hardware element. Two symmetrical hardware modules perform duplicate operations in lockstep (or delayed by a fixed period) and the results are compared. Any mismatch results in an error condition signaled by an alarm
This safety mechanism is enabled after application reset.

After a cold reset lockstep is enabled by default. The LSEN bit in the LCLCON register may be cleared during the boot to disable lockstep mode. SMU lockstep fault reporting should be disabled when lockstep is disabled
If this safety mechanism detects a fault, the CPU generates a CPU TriCore lockstep mismatch alarm.
SM[HW]:CPU:TRICORE_LOCKSTEP_SCC

The lockstep incorporates a hardware-based self-test. The self-test function sequentially injects faults into each comparator node. The lockstep detects that the fault was routed to the correct comparator node. If the fault was not routed to the correct comparator node or a real fault was detected, the lockstep generates an alarm
This safety mechanism is enabled after application reset.
The lockstep self-test function is enabled or disabled along with the lockstep function.
If this safety mechanism detects a fault, the CPU generates a CPU TriCore lockstep mismatch alarm.
SM[HW]:FB:ADDRESS

The SRAM implements a mechanism to monitor the address generation logic. This mechanism detects failures due to wrong address generation or decoding during read and write operations. This is achieved by providing each SRAM with a unique signature for each word, which is used to identify the word during read or write accesses. The signature is used to verify that an intended address is accessed   during a read or write operation by calculating the expected signature and comparing against the stored signature
An application reset will set the configuration of this safety mechanism back to its default value
Is active after application reset
Upon detection of a mismatch, a VMT Uncorrectable Error alarm will be reported to the SMU. In addition this is reflected in ECCD.UCERR
SM[HW]:FB:CONTROL

The VMT (volatile memory test) implements a detection of the following conditions of the surrounding logic of the SRAM SSH test mode gets enabled by a fault (or by setting the MEMTEST register) therefore the functional access of the SSH memories are not accessible (read or writable) SSH of security sensitive is enabled by a fault (or by setting the MEMTEST register for security sensitive SSHs) therefore clearing/auto-initializing the memories is executed and they are not accessible Triggering of MBIST FSM, by a fault (or by setting MCONTROL.START) Triggering of Test features in the data path, leading to potential data corruption by a fault (or by activating LBIST)
This mechanism is active after application reset
After detection of any of these events, a VMT Uncorrectable error alarm will be reported to the SMU. In addition this is reflected as critical operational error in FAULTSTS.OPERR
SM[HW]:FB:ERROR_CORRECTION
The SRAM implements a SEC-DED ECC logic. This mechanism detects SBE and DBE during read operations, SBE is also corrected if ECCS.ECE is set
The correction of single bit errors is enabled by setting the ECCS.ECE bit.

Upon correction of an SBE a VMT Correctable Error alarm will be send to the SMU. Upon detection of an DBE or ECE is disabled and SBE is detected a VMT Uncorrectable Error alarm will be send to the SMU
SM[HW]:FB:ECC_MONITOR
The SRAM implements a redundant hardware implementation of the ECC logic to detect faults in the Error Correction. This mechanism calculates the ECC codes in parallel and compares the results
This mechanism is active after application reset.
Upon detection of a mismatch, a VMT Uncorrectable Error alarm will be send to the SMU
SM[HW]:FB:ERROR_DETECTION
The SRAM implements a DED ECC logic. This mechanism detects SBE and DBE during read operations
This mechanism is active after application reset
Upon detection of an SBE or double-bit error , a VMT Uncorrectable Error alarm will be reported to the SMU
SM[HW]:FB:MBIST

AURIX TC4xx implements a destructive MBIST algorithm that offers a higher permanent hardware random fault diagnostic coverage than the former non-destructive inversion test (NDIT). This provides the ability to detect structural problems of the SRAMs and to detect permanent random hardware faults in memory cells and memory controller.
The application software shall execute the destructive MBIST test at least once per multi-point fault detection time interval (MPFDTI) on all SRAMs that are used by the application software
During the key-on MBIST phase, critical errors are reported through the ECCD register. Errors detected during the key-off MBIST phase are reported by the MSTATUS and RDBFL register
SM[HW]:FB:SM_CONTROL

The VMT implements the detection of the following events of the surrounding logic of the SRAM: • Any of the alarm notifications of UCENE or CENE got disabled • Any of the safety mechanisms got disabled (ECE, TRE, SBEm, DBE, ADDRE, OPENE)
This mechanism is active after application reset.
After detection of any of these events, a VMT Miscellaneous Error alarm will be reported to the SMU. In addition this is reflected as error in FAULTSTS.MISCERR.
SM[HW]:FB:RAM_REG_MONITOR

The FB implements safety flip-flops for detecting transient or permanent faults in the configuration registers and sequential logic. Safety flip-flops are special flip-flops that implement a hardware mechanism capable to detect bit flip within the flip-flops The configuration and control registers which are designed with safety flipflops are listed below: • MC(CS)i_ECCS (protected bit-fields: MENE, ECCMAP, SFLE, BFLE) • MC(CS)i_ALMSRCS (protected bit-field: MISCE)
Depends on the register default value
NA
After the monitor detects a fault, an alarm is send from SSH to VMT which forwards it to the SMU as VMT Uncorrectable Error Alarm
SM[HW]:MCU:LBIST

This is the "key-on" LBIST LBIST is an AURIX™ 3rd Generation platform safety mechanism which is based on an automatic structural system-test method. It checks the MCU digital logic for Hard Errors: latent and multi-point faults. The SM[HW]:MCU:LBIST is focusing exclusively on latent and multi-point faults within functional safety hardware structures. Its reduced volume allows a significant faster execution compared to a "key-off" LBIST, which targets the full digital design area
power-on reset will set the configuration of this safety mechanism back to its default value

SM[HW]:MCU:LBIST can be triggered automatically through the firmware after a cold power-on reset if this is configured in the UCB. If not triggered automatically it has to be configured and started through the Application SW by using the LBIST_CTRL_... registers The Application SW shall configure LBIST parameters via LBIST_CTRL_CFGA and start LBIST execution via LBIST_CTRL_REQ
LBIST alarm2): This alarm signal will be activated if the LBIST state machine is not in the Reset or Idle state, or if some global LBIST enable signals are active
SM[HW]:SCU:REG_MONITOR
The SCU implements safety flip-flops for detecting transient or permanent faults in the configuration registers and sequential logic.
This Safety mechanism is reset according to the reset behavior of each of the registers
NA
Upon detection of an error, an alarm will be reported to the SMU
SM[HW]:WTU:TEMPORAL_MONITORING

The WTU provides one dedicated watchdog timer (WDT) for each implemented CPU, for example CPU0 has WDTCPU0, CPU1 has WDTCPU1, etc, and one system WDT that is not assigned to a specific CPU and can be used by application software
This SM is reset by an application reset. WDTCPU0 and WDTSYS are enabled by the SYS_FW after a reset.
Many configuration in regsiters
On detection of a WDT overflow or access error events an alarm is signaled to the SMU through the "WTU x WDT overflow or access error alarm" (x = CPU0-5, CPUcs, System, combined
